coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence microspheres for ultrasensitive detection of malachite green in fish samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qin
Mao, Qiuyan
Cui, Yikun
Gong, Shiyun
Xiao, Lixia
Gong, Xiaojuan
Guan, Tianzhu
Yang, Zhen-quan
coredata.dc:description: This work made the first attempt to construct a florescence assay based on CDs@FMs (carbon dots-based fluorescence microspheres) for ultrasensitively probing malachite green (MG). Through static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE), the 581-nm emission peak of CDs@FMs could be significantly suppressed by MG. Our results demonstrated two good linear fluorescence responses for MG in the concentration scopes of 0.01–0.1 μM and 0.1–10.0 μM, respectively. Strikingly, the detection limits (LODs) achieved were down to picometer (56.7 pM) and nanometer levels (0.57 nM). Furthermore, this method exhibited good consistency with the HPLC results when applied for quantitatively screening MG from fish samples. Our current research highlights a promising application of CDs@FMs for enhanced MG probing in aquatic products with the merits of rapidity, simplicity, high accuracy and stable signal readout.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence microsphere
Carbon dots
Malachite green
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MG determination in fish samples by CDs@FMs
MG determination in fish samples by HPLC
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed sensing system
Discussion of the mechanism for MG detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM micrograph of CDs@FMs. (B) The XRD pattern of CDs@FMs. (C) The HRTEM image of CDs@FMs. (D) A magnification image of the HRTEM measurement of CDs@FMs species.

(A) The XPS spectra of CDs@FMs. (B) Spectra of UV–vis absorption (blue curve), excitation (red curve) and emission (black curve) for CDs@FMs. The inset shows CDs@FMs under light (left) and under UV irradiation (right). (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@FMs under different excitation wavelengths. (D) Fluorescent micrograph for CDs@FMs under 564-nm laser excitation.

(A) The PL spectra of CDs@FMs varied by MG dosase (from top to bottom): 0.038 μM. (B) The fitting curves of F0/F against a MG dosage range of 0.01–0.1 μM. (C)The fitting curves of F0/F against a MG dosage range of 0.1–10.0 μM.

(A) F0/F values for 0.07 mg mL−1 CDs@FMs solution incorporated with MG (10.0 μM) and diverse probable interfering substances (10.0 μM). (B) F0/F values for CDs@FMs solution (0.07 mg mL−1)/MG (10.0 μM) mixture with or without diverse interfering species (100 μM).

(A) Spectrum of UV–vis absorption (blue curve) for MG, as well as the spectra of excitation (green curve) and emission (red curve) for CDs@FMs. These spectra were normalised for ease of comparison. (B) Fluorescence decay plots for CDs@FMs with or without MG (10.0 μM) incorporation. (C) Spectra of UV–vis absorption for CDs@FMs in the presence of varying MG dosages (1.0–20.0 μM).
Illustrations for CDs@FMs fabrication and its fluorescence quenching process by MG.
Analytical performance comparison among various fluorescence microsphere probes for sensing MG.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: B-RCDs: blue-emission carbon dots (BCDs) and red-emission carbon dots (RCDs)
CDs&ZIF-8@MIPs: a novel molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor with a core–shell structure
CdTe-MIP/SiO2 NPs: a novel core–shell MIPs doped CdTe quantum dots coated silica nanoparticles
FS-CQDs: prepared from grass fish scales as precursors via a one-step hydrothermal approach to detect malachite green
SDS: acetonit rile–sodium dodecyl sulfate solution
LC-MS/MS: liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry
NCQDs: nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MG detection findings in fish samples by using our fluorescence method and HPLC (n=5).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescence microspheres for ultrasensitive detection of malachite green in fish samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work made the first attempt to construct a florescence assay based on CDs@FMs (carbon dots-based fluorescence microspheres) for ultrasensitively probing malachite green (MG). Through static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE), the 581-nm emission peak of CDs@FMs could be significantly suppressed by MG. Our results demonstrated two good linear fluorescence responses for MG in the concentration scopes of 0.01–0.1 μM and 0.1–10.0 μM, respectively. Strikingly, the detection limits (LODs) achieved were down to picometer (56.7 pM) and nanometer levels (0.57 nM). Furthermore, this method exhibited good consistency with the HPLC results when applied for quantitatively screening MG from fish samples. Our current research highlights a promising application of CDs@FMs for enhanced MG probing in aquatic products with the merits of rapidity, simplicity, high accuracy and stable signal readout.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite being a triarylmethane dye widely applied as efficacious parasiticide and fungicide in aquaculture, malachite green (MG) is reported to be highly toxic. After being transferred to human body via food chain, the fat-soluble MG with bio-accumulative property could potentially raise the risk of carcinogenicity, teratogenicity and mutagenicity (Chen and Miao, 2010). Therefore, many countries including the United States, Canada and China, have explicitly banned the MG application in aquaculture, specifying that no detectable amount of MG is allowed in aquatic products. Hence, achieving easy MG detection is imperative for food and environmental security. However, due to its advantages in low cost and high efficiency in curing water mildew, no effective substitute is available and the illegitimate MG utilisation still occurs repeatedly worldwide in fisheries, especially in the process of transportation. As such, developing a fast, economical and sensitive MG detection technique is highly demanded, so that food safety and human health can be ensured.

To date, diverse analytical strategies have been established for identifying MG, such as immunoassay (Zhang et al., 2015), colorimetry (Heleyel and Elhami, 2019), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Sun et al., 2017), electrochemical detection (ED) (Guo et al., 2017), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Li et al., 2017b), capillary electrophoresis (Maxwell and Tong, 2016), liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Liu and Liu, 2007), spray-mass spectrometry (Wei et al., 2018), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Zong and Lu, 2014) and molecular imprinting (Lin et al., 2019). Although these strategies had high sensitivity and accuracy; however, most of these techniques have the difficulties in sophisticated instrumentation, high cost, time-consuming processing and/or skilled specialists to operate. Specifically, the fluorescence methods, especially the recently sought-after carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescence probes, stand out due to its appealing advantages of rapidity, high sensitivity, shorter detection period and easy operation.

As quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles, the fluorescence CDs have diameters below 10 nm (Rahimi et al., 2019). Due to their fascinating optical and physicochemical traits, the CDs have been widely applied in various fields, especially for sensing applications. Researches showed that the CDs has found their important place in food colorants sensing, including MG (Cai et al., 2023), tartrazine (Liu et al., 2022), carmine (Su et al., 2018), auramine O (Wang et al., 2023a), allura red (Gunjal et al., 2021), etc. More importantly, integrating signal amplification strategy in CDs sensing applications for furthest enhance the attracted detection sensitivity attracted wide attention. The most popular pathway to gain signal amplification is to form fluorescence microspheres through fluorophore incorporation or labeling onto the surface of a specific kind of microspheres (Zhou et al., 2021). For example, Qiao et al. (2018) realized fluorescence signal amplification by using CDs as sensing element and silica nanoparticles as signal amplification carrier through a reverse microemulsion route, achieving a LOD of 0.07 nM for Cu2+ sensing. Wang et al. (2023b) developed a platform for amplifying signals through the CDs anchoring into melamine formaldehyde microspheres (MFMs), and achieved ultrasensitive auramine O identification, which realized sensitivity enhancing for AO detection, displaying a 0.07 nM limit of detection (LOD). Through the CDs loading into MFMs, Qin et al. (2021) formulated a signal amplification scheme for high sensitive detection of Au3+ and obtained a LOD of 31.0 nM, which put forwarded a valid strategy for amplifying fluorescence signals. However, the researches in CDs-based fluorescence microspheres (CDs@FMs) are still rare, and there is no report on using CDs@FMs for MG detection.

In the present study, an ultrasensitive florescence method by using CDs@FMs as probe was for the first time developed for MG sensing. The CDs was fabricated by hydrothermally heating rhodamine B while the CDs@FMs was prepared by incorporating CDs into MFMs ( Scheme 1). In the fluorescence assay, IFE and static interaction between CDs@FMs and MG occurred, leading to the quenching of CDs@FMs fluorescence as signal output. The process variables (e.g. pH, probe dosage and reaction duration) were optimized. Method validation was well conducted through examination of its linear range, LOD, selectivity and interference resistance. The mechanisms between CDs@FMs and MG were thoroughly investigated. Finally, our proposed approach was applied to MG identification in five kinds of fish samples, thereby confirming its feasibility for real sample analysis. To our best knowledge, this is the first study concerning CDs@FMs application as MG sensing probe, which had broad application prospects in the fields of food colorants monitoring.

Malachite green, anhydrous ethanol, rhodamine B dye, melamine and were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents were analytically pure or above and used directly devoid of further purification. Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) was the supplier of fructose, glucose, 37.0–40.0 % formaldehyde, all amino acids, 36.0–38.0 % hydrochloric acid, as well as diverse inorganic salts. All the rest reagents were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology (Shanghai).

The morphology of CDs@FMs was characterised using a Gemini scanning electron micro-scope (SEM) 300 (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was implemented with a Varian 670-IR + 610-IR FTIR system (Lincolnshire, IL, USA). A vario EL cube (Elementar, Hanau, Germany) was utilised to carry out elemental analysis. The fluorescence data and the UV−vis absorption data were gathered separately on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer and a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (both Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence microscopy images were captured with a LSM 880 NLO fluorescence microscopy (Carl Zeiss, Germany). A Zetasizer nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK) was utilised for determining zeta potential (ζ). The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was performed on a GeminiSEM 300 system (Carl Zeiss, Germany) using X-Max 8 Detection. The fluorescence decays were plotted by using a FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, England). An Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) was adopted for purifying ultrapure water.

Firstly, CDs was synthesised and purified as per a priorly reported work (Hu et al., 2021). Specifically, the CDs was purified by dialysis against 1.0 L ultrapure water for 3 days, during which fresh ultrapure water was recharged at a interval of 24 h with constant magnetic stirring applied. Then, the CDs@FMs were fabricated following a somewhat modified procedure described previously (Yuan et al., 2020). In detail, 2.52 g of melamine, 24.36 mL of 30 % formaldehyde solution and 25.64 mL of ultrapure water were mixed together. Then, the mixture solution was subjected to 10 min of stirring at 60 °C to form a transparent MF pre-polymer solution. Afterwards, the MF pre-polymer solution (7.0 mL) was blended with the CDs solution (0.22 mL, 2.6 mg mL−1) and HCl (23.4 mL, 0.4 mM). After stirring at 100 °C for 30 min, a MF-microsphere suspension yellow in colour was obtained. Following 10 min of centrifugation at 6000 rpm, the precipitate was gathered and rinsed in abundant water to remove the impurities. Last step was the freeze-drying of purified CDs@FMs solution for long-term storage.

Firstly, our approach was subjected to linearity assessment. Ultrapure water was used to prepare CDs@FMs solutions, whose concentration was 0.07 mg mL−1. In detail, CDs@FMs solution (2.0 mL) was taken and then various amounts of MG were gradually introduced to mix. The final amount of MG addition varied between 0.0110 μM. Under an excitation wavelength at 564-nm, the 581-nm fluorescence intensities were determined for the MG-involving CDs@FMs solutions. The LOD was estimated following the 3σ rule (3σ/KSV), with σ denoting the standard deviation in twelve replicate measurements for the CDs@FMs blank solution.

Afterwards, our approach was subjected to selectivity evaluation through observation of CDs@FMs fluorescence signal response towards MG, as well as diverse interfering substances. The possible interfering species include 6 kinds of metal ions (K+,Mg2+,Na+,Zn2+,Ca2+,Fe3+), 7 kinds of anionic ions (Br−,I−,F−,Cl−,NO3 −,H2PO4 −,HCO3 −), 8 kinds of amino acids (β-hydroxyalanine, L-methionine, histidine, threonine, alanine, DL-Cysteine, glycine, glutamic acid), as well as several small molecules (D-fructose, sucrose, glucose). In brief, 2.0 μL of a MG solution (10.0 mM) and the solutions of above-mentioned interfering species (10.0 mM) were separately blended with a CDs@FMs solution (2.0 mL, 0.07 mg mL−1). Each added substance was 10.0 µM in concentration. At a 564-nm excitation wavelength, the 581-nm fluorescence signals were determined for the CDs@FMs solution prior to and after mixing with the aforementioned substances.

Furthermore, the interference resistance of our approach was studied through the fluorescence response observation for a CDs@FMs/MG mixture towards MG and the aforementioned interfering species. Briefly, a CDs@FMs solution (2.0 mL) was added with 2.0 μL of a MG aqueous solution (10.0 mM). Then, 2.0 μL of the solutions of above-mentioned interfering species (100.0 mM) were separately blended to mix, making the final concentration of every interfering species at 100.0 µM. At a 564-nm excitation wavelength, the 581-nm fluorescence signals were determined for the CDs@FMs solution prior to and after mixing with the aforementioned substances.

Five types of fish samples, including grass carp, silver carp, snakehead, herring and common carp, were purchased from the local food markets in Yangzhou, China. Each 0.1 g of sample powder was weighed and placed inside a 50.0 mL centrifugal tube containing 15.0 mL of acetonitrile. After ultrasonication for 30 min to assist extraction, each sample was subjected to 10 min of centrifugation at 8000 rpm. Lastly, each collected supernatant was filtered via a polystyrene microporous filter (0.22 μM) and preserved at 4°C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MG determination in fish samples by CDs@FMs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a range of CDs@FMs solutions (each 2.0 mL, 0.07 mg mL−1) were formulated in 5.0 mL centrifuge tubes. Then, each 20.0 μL of the sample extraction was added and blended through manual shaking, separately. The fluorescence intensities were documented for CDs@FMs at 581-nm (λex=564-nm) prior to and following the sample extraction incorporation. Specifically, MG at three concentration levels of 0.1, 0.2 and 0.3 μM were added into each sample filtrate for the quantitative recovery evaluation. Each data was averaged from quintuplicate assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MG determination in fish samples by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: To validate accuracy of our proposed florescence method, all the fish samples were analysed by HPLC (Hu, Qin et al., 2021). Herein, an Agilent 1200 series HPLC (Santa Clara, CA, USA) provided with a photodiode array detector (PAD) was used to conduct the chromatographic analysis. A C18 stainless steel column (150×4.6 mm id; Waters Milford, MA, USA) was utilised to carry out the chromatographic separation under a 20.0 μL injection volume, a 1.0 mL min−1 flow rate and a 618-nm UV detection wavelength, where the mobile phase was composed of acetonitrile and 10.0 mM ammonium acetate (90:10, v/v). For every fish sample, five independent analyses were undertaken and their mean was taken as the MG concentration per sample.

The TEM image of CDs was firstly acquired to ensure the successful synthesis of CDs species. As shown in Fig. S1, the as-synthesized CDs species were spherical in shape and well distributed in a size range of 3.25.6 nm. The CDs@FMs were subjected to particle size and morphological characterisation by acquiring its XRD spectrum, SEM image and HRTEM image. As depicted in Fig. 1B, a shark peak at 2θ = 23.63° was showed in the XRD pattern of CDs@FMs, implying that the CDs@FMs has an amorphous carbon phase. The regular well-distributed regular spherical spots observed on the SEM image (Fig. 1 A) and HRTEM image (Fig. 1 C), which indicated the success formation of CDs@FMs with good dispersibility. A Nano Measurer software v1.2 was used to estimate the particle size information of CDs@MFs, and the CDs@MFs species were found to be distributed in a particle size range of 0.642.11 μm, whose mean size was 2.0 ± 0.5 μm. A partial magnification (Fig. S2A) of the SEM image of CDs@FMs species clearly showed the round shape surface structure. In addition, a magnification image of the HRTEM measurement (Fig. 1D) of CDs@FMs and the HRTEM image of MFMs (Fig. S2B) obviously showed the successful anchoring of CDs to MFMs.

The surface chemistry was studied by XPS, FTIR, EDS and ζ measurements. The XPS spectra of CDs@FMs were displayed in ( Fig. 2A).Three main predominant peaks at 287.1 eV (C1s), 398.1 eV (N1s) and 532.1 eV (O1s) were found in the full scan XPS spectrum of CDs@FMs (Fig. 2 A). The respective high-resolution spectra and the binding energy distribution of these peaks are shown in Figure. S3 (A) (C). The C1s spectrum (Fig. S3A) can be divided into two peaks at 284.5 eV (CC) and 287.0 eV (C−H). The N1s spectrum (Fig. S3B) showed clear peaks at 398.0 eV (C−N) and 399.1 eV (N−H). The XPS of O1s (Fig. S3C) can be split into two characteristic peaks of CO (531.8 eV) and C−O (532.6 eV). In FTIR spectrum of CDs@FMs (Fig. S4), the hydrophilic O–H/N–H groups underwent stretching vibrations (3340−3350 cm−1) on the surface of CDs@FMs, affording its good water solubility. The 2960 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 absorption bands corresponded separately to the stretching vibrations of C−H and CO. The absorptions at 1500 cm−1 and 1354 cm−1 were attributed to C–H and CC bonds, separately. The 1165 cm−1 and 1009 cm−1 absorptions were ascribed separately to the C−N and C−O tensile vibrations. The FTIR analysis were in well consistency with the XPS measurement. For comparison, the FTIR data for CDs was obtained (Fig. S4). Absorption bands of N−H/O−H, C−H, CO, CC, C−N and C−O groups that were severally found at 3346, 2960, 1560, 1354, 1165 and 1009 cm−1 clearly implied the successful anchoring of CDs species onto the MFMs. As illustrated by EDS measurement in Fig. S5, the average atomic ratio O in CDs@FMs was 11.42 % while the average atomic ratio N was as high as 47.21 %, which indicated that the number of N atoms on the surface of CDs@FMs is about ten times that of O atoms. As shown in Fig. S6, the ζ of the free-state CDs was +4.08 mV. After anchoring to MFMs, the resulted CDs-based florescence microspeheres, i.e., CDs@FMs, had more positive charges with a much higher ζ of +31.5 mV. This observation was in consistence with the EDS measurement since three are a large number of N atoms on CDs@FMs surface.

Through PL spectroscopy combined with UV–vis absorption, we studied the optical traits for the as-prepared CDs@FMs. According to the UV−vis pattern (Fig. 2B, blue curve), The CDs@FMs had in total four typical absorption bands. The two short-wavelength absorption bands were 261-nm and 302-nm, which were ascribed to the CN bond ππ* transition (Jiao et al., 2018) and the CO bond nπ* transition (Gu et al., 2020), respectively. Another two absorption bands at long-wavelength range were 409-nm and 559-nm, attributing to N−H and C−N groups (Zhang et al., 2021) and the low-energy surface state (Pan et al., 2016), respectively. The excitaion spectrum of CDs@FMs (Fig. 2B, red curve) showed that it had a maximal excitation wavelength at 564-nm. Upon excitation at 564-nm, a 581-nm emission peak appeared for CDs@FMs (Fig. 2B, black curve). In addition, the change of PL property of CDs@FMs with excitation wavelength was studied. As shown in Fig. 2 C, the CDs@FMs displayed an excitation-independent behaviour in varying excitation wavelength settings. With a 480–to–565-nm rise in excitation wavelength, the emission band position of CDs@FMs was locked at around 581-nm with the maximum emission intensity achieved at an excitation of 564-nm. As such, the best excitation wavelength for sensing task was set to 564-nm. Furthermore, a fluorescence microscopic image of CDs@FMs was acquired under a 546-nm laser excitation. As shown in Fig. 2D, bright orange emission was visualized. Given the fact that the MFMs structure were non-fluorescent, the observation of fluorescent emission unambiguously substantiated the successful combination of CDs species to non-fluorescent MFMs structure and formation of CDs-based fluorescent microspheres, i.e., CDs@FMs.

To promote the detectability, we firstly investigated the effects of significant parameters (including pH, sensing element concentration and reaction time) on the F0/F ratio (fluorescence intensity of CDs@FMs prior to the MG incorporation as a percentage of that following the MG incorporation). Fig. S7A depicted the F0/F variations with the dosage of CDs@FMs. The F0/F fluctuated significantly as the CDs@FMs dosage increased from 0.0 to 1.0 mg mL−1, whose value was the highest when the concentration of CDs@FMs was at 0.07 mg mL−1. Therefore, the optimal dosage of CDs@FMs for MG detection was set at 0.07 mg mL−1.

The change of F0/F with pH was illustrated in Fig. S7B. All the pH environments (3.012.0) were offered by 10.0 mM PBS apart from pH 5.8, which was offered by ultrapure water. With the rise in pH from 3.0 to 12.0, alteration of F0/F was noted, revealing a highest value when the pH was 5.8 (ultrapure water). Accordingly, the ultrapure water was finally chosen to prepare the CDs@FMs working solutions.

The influence of reaction time on F0/F was finally investigated (Fig. S7C). Drastic elevation in F0/F was noted following a 1.0 min reaction of MG with CDs@FMs, which was kept almost stable over a longer reaction duration, suggesting that a short 1.0 min was enough for completing the reaction between MG and CDs@FMs. As such, 1.0 min was chosen as the best duration of reaction for MG sensing.

The application of CDs@FMs in fluorescent sensing of MG was demonstrated by acquiring the PL spectra of CDs@FMs after mixing with various amounts of MG. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the additions of MG (0.038.0 μM) to CDs@FMs led to gradual fluorescence quenching. The CDs@FMs exhibited fluorescence intensity weakening by about 50 % after the incorporation of 8.5 μM MG, and the incorporation of 38.0 μM, 2200 % of quenching efficiency was observed. Then, the Stern-Volmer formula (F0/F=1+K
SV [Conc.], with [Conc.] denoting the MG content and K
SV referring to calibration slop) was used to study the quenching behaviour of MG on CDs@FMs. As exhibited in Figs. 3B and 3 C, two good linear relationships (0.01–0.1 μM and 0.1–10.0 μM) were observed between MG concentration and F0/F. The corresponding linear regression equations were F0/F=1.6039[C]+0.9887 and F0/F=0.1596[C]+1.1587, separately. The K
SV estimates were 1.6039×106 and 0.1596×106 L mol−1, with the correlation coefficients (R2) being 0.9932 and 0.9946, separately. The LOD estimates for MG according to 3σ/K
SV (σ denotes the standard deviation of control) were 56.7 pM and 0.57 nM, separately. The proposed method created the first record of a picometer-level LOD in terms of using CDs as probe for MG detection. As illustrated in Table 1, the LODs of the proposed probe were far lower than any of the existing fluorescence methods. It is particularly noteworthy that compared to the published SERS and HPLC methods based on advanced modern analytical instruments, our developed method exhibited more than one order of magnitude lower LODs. The results mentioned above fully prove the potential of using CDs@FMs for MG detection with high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Through selectivity exploration, the feasibility of CDs@FMs for detecting MG was firstly assessed. The CDs@FMs fluorescence response towards MG, as well as probable interfering species were recorded and depicted in Fig. 4 A and Fig. S8. After the addition of MG and the other interferants at a concentration of 10.0 μM, only MG caused noticeable quenching of CDs@FMs fluorescence intensity, while the above-described interferants did not have significant influence on the signal intensity, suggesting that the CDs@FMs have appreciable selectivity towards MG detection.

Anti-interference ability is another crucial factor that affects the practical sensing application of a probe. The anti-interference ability of CDs@FMs towards MG was examined by adding the above-described interferants to a CDs@FMs (0.07 mg mL−1)/MG (10.0 μM) mixture solution, respectively. As is clear from Fig. 4B, CDs@FMs did not exhibit obvious change in fluorescence intensity after adding interfering substances, indicating that the CDs@FMs had good interference resistance for sensing MG. In essence, our sensing system has the potential for MG sensing in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion of the mechanism for MG detection
ce:section.ce:para: Spectral studies are common and highly efficient tools in clarifying florescence quenching mechanism. Herein, the spectrum of UV–vis absorption for MG ( Fig. 5 A, blue curve), along with the spectra of excitation (Fig. 5 A, green curve) and emission (Fig. 5 A, red curve) for CDs@FMs were initially obtained. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the blue curve exhibits a high degree of overlap with the green and red curves, indicating the possibility of inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Since the distance between CDs@FMs and MG tended to be greater than 10 nm due to the electrostatic repulsion between CDs@FMs (ζ=+31.5 mV, Fig. S6) and MG (ζ=+3.7 mV, Fig. S6), which ruled out the possibility of FRET. As such, it was inferred that IFE was one of the mechanism that led to the florescence quenching of CDs@FMs by MG. Then, fluorescence decays were plotted for CDs@FMs after mixing with varying MG dosages (Fig. 5B). The fitted outcomes based on a one-exponential function were illustrated in Table S1. The fluorescence lifetimes before and after the addition of MG were 1.23 and 1.19 ns, respectively. The interaction between CDs@FMs and MG led to nearly no change of fluorescence lifetime of CDs@FMs, suggesting the existence of static quenching. Furthermore, the attempts were dedicated to examine whether the fluorescence intensity alteration in CDs@FMs was the result of new compound generation. Fig. 5 C depicted the spectra of UV–vis absorption for CDs@FMs incorporated with various MG amounts. After MG blending into CDs@FMs, the representative MG absorption bands at 260, 310, 430 and 620-nm appeared and the intensity increased with the increase of MG content and no obvious shifts of the absorption bands were found, which excluded from the reason for the formation of new species between CDs@FMs and MG. In essence, it is believed that the quenching of of CDs@FMs florescence was caused by IFE and static interaction between CDs@FMs and MG.

To evaluate the practicality of our analytical approach in actual application, we detected MG contents in aforementioned five types of fish samples. As is clear from Table 2, no MG was traced in any of these samples. The spiked recovery rates of the proposed method were 95.1 %–104.5 % with the standard deviations (RSDs) were below 5.65 %. To further check the analysis accuracy, the HPLC method was applied. The chromatograms were depicted in Fig. S9. The consistent analytical results between the two methods indubitable demonstrated the high accuracy of our proposed fluorescence method.

In short, a rapid and ultra-sensitive method based on CDs@FMs towards MG sensing was developed in this work. The CDs@FMs were fabricated by incorporating CDs into the MFMs, which acted as signal amplification carrier. Very good linearity was observed for identifying MG over two dosages range of 0.01–0.10 μM. In addition, benefited from the amplification function, the proposed CDs@FMs probe achieved an ultrahigh sensitivity with a LOD as low as 56.7 pM. The fluorescence detecting mechanism was grounded on signal suppressing at 581-nm via IFE and static interaction between CDs@FMs and MG. We succeeded in verifying the established analytical approach for sensing MG in five kinds of real fish samples. The detection results were in consistent with that achieved from HPLC analyses and the recoveries were also satisfactory, which validated the reliability of the newly developed method. The results implied that involving CDs@FMs with function of signal amplification for MG sensing was a useful tool for sensitivity enhancement, which would doubtlessly broaden the application scope of CDs-based probes for analyzing foods.

Shiyun Gong: Methodology, Formal analysis. Lixia Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Qiuyan Mao: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yikun Cui: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhenquan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Xiaojuan Gong: Methodology, Formal analysis. Tianzhu Guan: Methodology, Formal analysis. Qin Hu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: On behalf of all the authors that contribute to this manuscript, I declare there is no interest of conflict for the work present in this paper. In addition, the paper is unpublished and has not been submitted for publication elsewhere.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949) and LvYang Golden Phoenix Plan, Yangzhou City (2019) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.106497.
